text: A large piece of retroperitoneal fat is visible, previously hidden within the spermatic cord.
The surgeon works in the space of Bogros, dissecting through the natural tissue plane and approaching the peritoneum laterally. An additional port is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a Cartier instrument for retraction while working around the spermatic cord, revealing a significant amount of fat within the cord structures. The fat is visible anterior to the cord structures as the surgeon considers the next steps.
The surgeon identifies an abnormally abundant column of fat within the scrotal hernia, noting the need for its reduction. Despite expectations, the hernia sac appears smaller than anticipated.

The surgeon works around the spermatic cord structures near the internal ring, attempting to reduce the abundant fat. A fascial layer is visibly opened, while the bedside assistant compresses the scrotum to aid in reducing the hernia contents.
The surgeon identifies a clear plane between the anterior yellowish fat and the underlying structure with multiple long vessels. The surgeon proceeds to divide the fat at a visually distinct point.

The surgeon, with the assistance of a bedside assistant, attempts to further reduce the contents of the scrotum by pulling gently on the structures. The hernia sac appears to be completely reduced, and a suitable point for division is identified.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the hernia sac, prioritizing the spermatic cord. The tip of the hernia sac is visible medially, and the vas deferens is expected to come into view soon. The medial dissection in the space of Retzius is not shown, focusing instead on the large piece of retroperitoneal fat within the spermatic cord.
The surgeon continues to parietalize the spermatic cord, revealing the vas deferens. The cord appears enlarged, suggesting the possibility of a hidden cord lipoma.
The surgeon examines the surgical field for any fat that appears different from the visible yellow fat in front of them, highlighting a distinct yellow piece of fat.
The surgeon carefully dissects around a piece of fat that appears intimately involved with the testicular vessels, ensuring there is no sliding component involving the cecum or pericycle fat.
The surgeon examines a structure that appears to be separating from the testicular vessels, potentially explaining the palpable mass observed during the physical exam.

The surgeon carefully maintains hemostasis while dissecting to separate the testicular vessels from surrounding fat. The fat appears amorphous and breaks apart easily, lacking a distinct plane of separation from the vessels.

The surgeon manipulates the layers of fat, observing a potential natural division point while ensuring no large testicular vessels are visible. A larger amount of fat is reduced, with careful consideration to avoid damaging the genital femoral nerve.

The surgeon dissects a piece of fat, which becomes narrower and appears as a separate entity. A significant amount of fat is left along the core structures, and additional fat is observed in the patient's scrotum, along with the hernia sac.

The surgeon continues to dissect medially, observing a column of fat that cannot be separated from the testicular vessels. The anterior dissection appears complete, but additional reducible fat is noted posteriorly.
The surgeon applies gentle pressure in various directions to manipulate the tissue, assisted by a bedside assistant. Suddenly, a large quantity of tissue is visibly reduced from the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies a large piece of tissue previously palpated during the physical exam and searches for a distal transection point to separate it from the testicular vessels. The wad of fat is visible as the surgeon approaches its tip.

The surgeon manipulates a large mass of fat, searching for a point to separate it from the testicular vessels. The aim is to isolate the cord structures and ensure the testicular vessels are not compromised during the dissection.

The surgeon carefully examines the surgical field, dividing small vessels while retracting and pushing tissue in various directions. A small amount of tissue remains to be divided, which, once separated, will allow the piece of fat to detach from the testicular vessels.

The surgeon uses a bipolar device to manage small vessels that retract quickly. A significant amount of tissue is visibly reduced during the last pull, with the bedside assistant confirming that most of the contents have been reduced.

The surgeon continues to separate excess fat tissue from the testicular vessels to create a clear landing zone for mesh placement, ensuring that the mesh will not be compromised by tissue herniating into the indirect space.
The surgeon attempts to separate a piece of fat from the testicular vessels, carefully dissecting layer by layer to visualize the inguinal canal and ensure no additional fat remains.
The surgeon identifies a natural plane to thin out the testicular vessels, ensuring the bulk of the fat does not slide behind the mesh. A lymphatic channel is visible in the surgical field.
A large vein becomes visible, prompting the surgeon to adjust the dissection path. The surgeon moves anteriorly to preserve the prominent vein, which is located to the left of the scissors.

The surgeon navigates around a tortuous vein hidden within the surrounding fat, carefully repositioning it to avoid transection. The fat is completely separated and moved cephalad, revealing the vein's path.

The surgeon has preserved a large vein while skeletonizing the core structures, creating a landing zone approximately eight centimeters away from the internal ring for the placement of a sixteen twelve Progrip mesh.

The surgeon tucks the mesh deep into the space of Retzius, ensuring it covers the anterior portion of the defect. The small to medium hernia sac is visible, and the flap is used to patch the defect in the preperitoneal space.
